usageNotes: Not the chubby baby angels of Renaissance art. Biblical cherubim are powerful, awe-inspiring beings. Ezekiel describes them with four faces and four wings.

definition: Plural of 'seraph'; heavenly beings with six wings who worship God around His throne, crying 'Holy, holy, holy.'
usageNotes: Appear only in Isaiah's temple vision. The name may mean 'burning ones.' They have six wings: two covering face, two covering feet, two for flying.

definition: The Hebrew term for the abode of the dead; the underworld or grave. A shadowy realm where the dead reside, distinct from the Christian concept of hell.
modernEquivalent: the grave / realm of the dead
usageNotes: Not exactly hell or heaven. Sheol is the general abode of the dead in Old Testament thought. Both righteous and wicked go there.

definition: The miraculous food God provided daily to the Israelites during their 40 years of wilderness wandering. White, sweet, and flake-like.
usageNotes: The name comes from the Hebrew 'man hu?' meaning 'What is it?' Appeared each morning with the dew. Could not be stored overnight except before the Sabbath.

definition: An extremely large, indefinite number; ten thousand in ancient counting.
usageNotes: Originally meant specifically ten thousand but came to mean any vast number. 'Ten thousand times ten thousand' is a myriad of myriads.

definition: News; a message bringing information, especially of events.
usageNotes: 'Good tidings of great joy' is the angelic announcement of Christ's birth. Always used with a sense of significance.

definition: A trick, stratagem, or crafty device intended to deceive or ensnare.
usageNotes: 'The wiles of the devil' describes Satan's crafty strategies. Usually plural: 'wiles.' Related to being 'beguiled.'

definition: Cunning, deceit, or craftiness; the quality of being treacherously clever.
usageNotes: A negative quality. 'In whose spirit there is no guile' describes an honest, transparently sincere person. Nathanael was praised for having no guile.

definition: Deep-seated hostility, hatred, or antagonism between parties.
usageNotes: God placed 'enmity' between the serpent and the woman in Genesis 3:15, a verse seen as the first messianic prophecy.

definition: Agreement, harmony, or unity between persons or things.
usageNotes: 'What concord hath Christ with Belial?' asks what agreement Christ has with the devil—the answer being none.

definition: A likeness, image, or comparison; the quality of being similar or a figure of resemblance.
usageNotes: Used in the sense of an image, form, or likeness. 'The similitude of the LORD' means the form or appearance of God.

definition: The face, facial features, or appearance of a person.
usageNotes: 'His visage was so marred' describes a face so disfigured as to be barely recognizable, referring prophetically to Christ's suffering.

definition: The greatest possible degree; the most extreme; the farthest point.
usageNotes: 'Able to save them to the uttermost' means able to save completely, to the fullest extent possible.

definition: A tall aquatic plant growing in marshes and along riverbanks. Baby Moses's basket was made of bulrushes.
usageNotes: Bulrushes (papyrus) grew abundantly along the Nile. They were used for making baskets, boats, and writing material.

definition: A loose, sleeveless outer cloak or garment. Symbolically represents authority or prophetic office.
usageNotes: Elijah's mantle was passed to Elisha, symbolizing the transfer of prophetic authority. 'Picking up the mantle' comes from this.

definition: A belt or sash worn around the waist to secure garments. Could also serve as a purse or holder.
usageNotes: Not the modern undergarment. A girdle was an essential garment for tucking up robes for work or travel. John the Baptist wore a leather girdle.

definition: The chief of a tribe; a tribal ruler.
usageNotes: Related to the division of Israel into twelve tribes, each led by a tribal head.

definition: Twelve loaves of bread placed each Sabbath on the golden table in the tabernacle before the LORD. Also spelled 'showbread.'
modernEquivalent: consecrated bread / bread of the Presence
usageNotes: Literally 'bread of the face' or 'bread of the Presence.' Only priests could eat it. David ate it in an emergency, cited by Jesus.

definition: A small hand drum or tambourine used in worship and celebration.
usageNotes: Miriam played the timbrel after crossing the Red Sea. Used in joyful worship and celebrations.

definition: A stringed musical instrument similar to a harp or zither, plucked with the fingers.
usageNotes: One of several instruments mentioned in temple worship. Often listed alongside the harp and timbrel.

definition: A musical instrument, possibly a type of bagpipe or stringed instrument. Its exact identification is debated.
usageNotes: Mentioned in Daniel among instruments commanded to be played when worshipping Nebuchadnezzar's golden image.

definition: A substance (especially yeast) that causes bread dough to rise. Metaphorically, an influence that spreads throughout something.
usageNotes: Used metaphorically by Jesus for the spreading influence of teaching (good or bad). 'Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees' warns against their hypocrisy.

definition: Sediment that settles at the bottom of wine; dregs. Figuratively, a state of complacency or stagnation.
usageNotes: 'Wine on the lees' is aged wine left on its sediment, considered finest quality. 'Settled on his lees' means become complacent.

definition: A large vessel for holding wine or other drink; a bottle or jug.
usageNotes: 'Stay me with flagons' in Song of Solomon is a request for refreshment. Flagons were containers for wine or raisin cakes.

definition: A small bag or wallet carried by travelers, shepherds, or pilgrims for provisions.
usageNotes: Jesus told his disciples to take no scrip (bag) for their journey, trusting God to provide through others' hospitality.

definition: Plural of 'staff'; walking sticks, poles, or rods. Also the poles used to carry the Ark of the Covenant.
usageNotes: The Ark of the Covenant was carried using staves inserted through gold rings. Also refers to walking sticks or shepherd's staffs.

definition: The smallest Roman coin, worth about one-fourth of a penny. Used to illustrate minimal value.
modernEquivalent: the smallest coin / a tiny amount
usageNotes: Used by Jesus to illustrate God's care for even the smallest creatures: not one sparrow falls without God's notice, and sparrows sold for two farthings.

definition: The smallest Jewish coin, worth half a farthing. Used to illustrate the value of sacrificial giving.
usageNotes: The widow's two mites, though nearly worthless monetarily, were praised by Jesus as the greatest offering because she gave all she had.

definition: A Roman silver coin representing a day's wage for a common laborer. The 'penny' in KJV.
usageNotes: The coin shown to Jesus in the 'render unto Caesar' exchange. Workers in the vineyard parable each received one denarius.

definition: A bundle of grain stalks bound together after reaping.
usageNotes: Joseph's dream of sheaves bowing down foreshadowed his brothers bowing to him. Gleaning leftover sheaves was how the poor gathered food.

definition: A vat or trough where grapes were crushed to extract juice for wine, typically by treading with bare feet.
usageNotes: Used literally of grape processing and metaphorically of God's judgment: treading the winepress of God's wrath in Revelation.

definition: A flat, hard surface where harvested grain was separated from its husks by beating or having animals tread on it.
usageNotes: Significant locations in the Bible. David purchased Araunah's threshing floor, which became the site of Solomon's Temple.

definition: The blade of a plow that cuts through the earth. Symbol of peaceful agriculture.
usageNotes: 'Beat their swords into plowshares' is a prophetic vision of converting weapons of war into tools of peaceful farming.

definition: A curved blade on a handle used for trimming grapevines and fruit trees.
usageNotes: Paired with 'plowshare' in the famous prophecy: 'spears into pruninghooks.' Represents tools of peaceful cultivation.

definition: To separate grain from chaff by tossing it in the air and letting the wind blow away the lighter chaff.
usageNotes: Used metaphorically of separating the worthy from the unworthy. John the Baptist said the Messiah would thoroughly purge his floor.

definition: The husks and debris separated from grain during winnowing. Figuratively, something worthless blown away by the wind.
usageNotes: 'The ungodly are like the chaff which the wind driveth away' is a vivid image of the impermanence and worthlessness of the wicked.

definition: An underground reservoir or tank for storing rainwater.
usageNotes: Jeremiah uses 'broken cisterns that can hold no water' as a metaphor for false gods that cannot satisfy. Cisterns were vital in arid Palestine.

definition: A weapon made of leather strips and a pouch, used to hurl stones at great speed and accuracy.
usageNotes: David killed Goliath with a sling and stone. Slingers were valued warriors in ancient armies. Benjamin's tribe was known for skilled slingers.

definition: A light spear designed for throwing. A common weapon in ancient warfare.
usageNotes: Saul threw his javelin at David multiple times in jealous rage. The javelin was distinct from the heavier battle spear.

definition: A case or container for holding arrows, typically slung over the shoulder or hung at the hip.
usageNotes: 'Happy is the man that hath his quiver full of them' compares children to arrows in a quiver—a blessed abundance.

definition: A young bull, especially one used for sacrifice or labor.
usageNotes: Bullocks were common sacrificial animals. Elijah vs. the prophets of Baal each used a bullock for their altar.

definition: A young cow that has not yet had a calf, sometimes used in sacrificial rituals.
usageNotes: The red heifer ceremony (Numbers 19) was a unique purification ritual. Samson's riddle refers to his wife as a heifer.

definition: An adult male deer, especially a red deer.
usageNotes: 'As the hart panteth after the water brooks' is a vivid image of deep spiritual longing in Psalm 42.

definition: A female deer, especially a red deer.
usageNotes: 'The hind of the morning' appears in the title of Psalm 22. Hinds represent grace, beauty, and sure-footedness.

definition: Made of brass or bronze. Also means bold or shameless.
usageNotes: The 'brazen serpent' was a bronze snake Moses set on a pole to heal snakebite victims. 'Brazen' usually means bronze in KJV.

definition: The process of being made holy; the work of God's grace by which a believer is set apart and progressively made more like Christ.
usageNotes: A key theological concept. Distinct from justification (being declared righteous), sanctification is the ongoing process of becoming holy in practice.

definition: The act of reckoning or crediting something to someone's account; in theology, God crediting Christ's righteousness to believers.
usageNotes: Abraham's faith was 'imputed' (credited) to him as righteousness. A foundational concept in Protestant theology of salvation.

definition: The restoration of a broken relationship; in theology, God restoring the relationship between Himself and humanity through Christ.
usageNotes: Believers are given the 'ministry of reconciliation'—the work of helping others be reconciled to God through Christ.

definition: The act of buying back or setting free by paying a price; in theology, Christ's payment for human sin through His death.
usageNotes: Draws on the imagery of slave markets and the kinsman-redeemer practice. Christ's blood is the redemption price for humanity.

definition: God's foreordaining or determining in advance who will be saved; the divine plan established before creation.
usageNotes: A debated theological concept. Paul writes that believers were 'predestinated' to be conformed to the image of God's Son.

definition: A period of time during which God deals with humanity in a particular way; also, the stewardship or administration of God's plan.
usageNotes: Paul speaks of 'the dispensation of the grace of God' given to him—his role as steward of the gospel message.

definition: Humble, earnest prayer or petition; a plea made with deep sincerity and urgency.
usageNotes: Stronger than ordinary prayer; implies deep need and humility. Often paired with 'prayer' in the phrase 'prayer and supplication.'

definition: Chosen by God; selected for salvation or a special purpose. As a noun, those whom God has chosen.
usageNotes: Christ is called God's 'elect' (chosen one). Believers are also called 'the elect.' The concept of election is central to Reformed theology.

definition: Spiritual rebirth; the divine act of giving new spiritual life to a person. Also used of the renewal of all things at the end of the age.
usageNotes: Jesus speaks of 'the regeneration' as a future cosmic renewal. Titus 3:5 uses it of individual spiritual rebirth through the Holy Spirit.

definition: The act of raising to a high position, honor, or power; in theology, Christ's glorification after His humiliation.
usageNotes: Christ's exaltation follows his humiliation: after death on a cross, God 'highly exalted him.' Also used of the humble being lifted up.

definition: A spoken blessing, especially at the end of a worship service. Literally, 'good speaking.'
usageNotes: The Aaronic benediction ('The LORD bless thee and keep thee') from Numbers 6:24-26 is the most famous biblical blessing formula.

definition: The part of a worship service where offerings are collected or presented; the offering itself.
usageNotes: Related to the practice of bringing gifts and offerings to the temple. In Christian worship, the time when gifts are collected.

definition: The forgiveness or cancellation of sins, debts, or penalties.
usageNotes: 'Without shedding of blood is no remission' establishes the necessity of blood sacrifice for forgiveness in the Old Covenant.

definition: Formal release from guilt, blame, or sin; a declaration of forgiveness.
usageNotes: Related to being absolved from sin. God's absolution is based on repentance and faith in Christ's atoning work.

definition: The abandonment or renunciation of a religious faith; falling away from previously held beliefs.
usageNotes: Paul warns of a great apostasy before Christ's return: 'a falling away' must come first. A serious concern in the epistles.

definition: One who acts as an intermediary between two parties to bring about agreement or reconciliation.
usageNotes: 'There is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.' Moses was mediator of the Old Covenant; Christ of the New.

definition: A person who has converted from one religion to another, especially a Gentile convert to Judaism.
usageNotes: Pharisees were zealous to make proselytes. Proselytes were Gentiles who fully embraced Judaism, including circumcision and Torah observance.

definition: Strong public criticism, verbal abuse, or disgrace.
usageNotes: Related to the reproach and shame heaped upon God's people or prophets by their enemies.

definition: A person who claims to foretell the future through supernatural means; a fortune-teller or diviner.
usageNotes: Soothsaying was strictly forbidden in Israel. Balaam is sometimes called a soothsayer. The practice is associated with pagan nations.

definition: An ancient unit of length based on the forearm, from elbow to fingertip, approximately 18 inches (45 cm).
usageNotes: Noah's ark was 300 cubits long (about 450 feet). Goliath was six cubits and a span tall. The standard unit of length in the ancient Near East.

definition: A Hebrew unit of dry measure equal to about 22 liters or roughly 3/5 of a bushel.
usageNotes: Used for measuring grain and flour. An ephah of flour was a standard offering quantity. Related to the bath (liquid measure of the same volume).

definition: A Hebrew unit of liquid measure equal to about 6 liters or roughly 1.5 gallons.
usageNotes: Used in prescriptions for drink offerings and anointing oil. Smaller than a bath (which equals about 6 hins).

definition: A unit of weight (about 11.5 grams) and the basic unit of silver currency in ancient Israel.
usageNotes: Used for weighing precious metals before coinage existed. 'Thirty shekels of silver' was the price Judas received for betraying Jesus, and the value of a slave in Exodus.

definition: The largest unit of weight in the ancient Near East, approximately 75 pounds (34 kg). Also a large sum of money.
modernEquivalent: about 75 pounds / large sum of money
usageNotes: In Jesus's parable, servants were entrusted with talents (huge sums). One talent of gold was worth about 20 years' wages. The modern word 'talent' (ability) comes from this parable.

definition: A unit of distance equal to one-eighth of a mile, or about 201 meters (660 feet).
usageNotes: Used in KJV to translate the Greek 'stadion.' Emmaus was 'threescore furlongs' (about 7 miles) from Jerusalem.

definition: A unit of depth measurement equal to 6 feet (1.83 meters), used especially in measuring water depth.
usageNotes: Used in Paul's shipwreck narrative: 'they sounded, and found it twenty fathoms' (120 feet deep).

definition: A unit of length equal to the distance from thumb tip to little finger tip when the hand is spread, about 9 inches (23 cm).
usageNotes: Half a cubit. Goliath's height included 'a span.' Jesus asked who can add one cubit to their stature by worrying.

definition: A Hebrew unit of liquid measure equal to about 22 liters (approximately 6 gallons), equivalent to the dry ephah.
usageNotes: Used for measuring wine, oil, and other liquids. Solomon's sea held 2,000 baths. One bath equals about six hins.

definition: A Hebrew unit of dry measure equal to about 220 liters or roughly 6 bushels. The largest dry measure.
usageNotes: Equal to ten ephahs. A homer of barley was a substantial quantity. Used in measuring large quantities of grain.

definition: A Hebrew unit of measure equivalent to a homer, about 220 liters for dry goods or liquids.
usageNotes: Solomon's daily provision included thirty cors of fine flour. Used interchangeably with homer.

definition: A Hebrew unit of weight equal to half a shekel, about 5.7 grams.
usageNotes: Each Israelite paid a bekah (half shekel) as a census offering. A small weight used for precious metals.

definition: The smallest Hebrew unit of weight, equal to one-twentieth of a shekel, about 0.57 grams.
usageNotes: Twenty gerahs make one shekel. Used to define the shekel's weight in Exodus and Ezekiel.

definition: A Hebrew unit of dry measure equal to one-tenth of an ephah, about 2.2 liters.
usageNotes: Each person gathered an omer of manna per day. The omer is also associated with the counting period between Passover and Pentecost.

definition: A professional copyist and interpreter of the Jewish law; a religious scholar and teacher of the Scriptures.
usageNotes: Scribes were experts in the Torah who copied, taught, and interpreted the law. Jesus often debated with them. Ezra was a notable scribe.

definition: A Jewish tax collector who worked for the Roman government, despised as a traitor and sinner by fellow Jews.
usageNotes: Publicans were hated for collaborating with Rome and often overcharging. Matthew (Levi) and Zacchaeus were publicans. Jesus was criticized for eating with them.

definition: A Roman provincial administrator or governor who managed financial and judicial affairs, like Pontius Pilate.
usageNotes: Pontius Pilate, Felix, and Festus were procurators (governors) of Judea, responsible for Roman administration and justice.

definition: A ruler of one-fourth of a region; a subordinate ruler under Roman authority governing part of a divided territory.
usageNotes: Herod Antipas was tetrarch of Galilee when Jesus was crucified. After Herod the Great's death, his kingdom was divided among his sons as tetrarchies.

definition: A Roman official serving as a military commander or provincial administrator.
usageNotes: Pontius Pilate's title may have been prefect rather than procurator. Prefects had military command authority in Roman provinces.

definition: A Roman governor of a senatorial province, answerable to the Roman Senate rather than the emperor.
usageNotes: Sergius Paulus and Gallio were proconsuls Paul encountered. The proconsul was the highest Roman authority in a senatorial province.

definition: A person entrusted with the management of another's household, property, or affairs; a caretaker or manager.
usageNotes: Jesus told parables about stewards. Christians are called stewards of God's grace—entrusted with managing what belongs to God.

definition: An overseer who assigns and enforces labor, especially harsh forced labor.
usageNotes: The Egyptian taskmasters oppressed the Israelites with harsh forced labor, leading to their cry for deliverance.

definition: The practice of foretelling the future through supernatural or occult means; divination.
usageNotes: Strictly forbidden in Israel. The slave girl in Philippi had 'a spirit of divination' (soothsaying) from which Paul freed her.

definition: A person appointed to distribute alms (charitable gifts) to the poor.
usageNotes: Related to the practice of almsgiving, which was considered a primary religious duty in Judaism and early Christianity.

definition: The male head of a family or tribal line; specifically, the founding fathers of the Israelite nation (Abraham, Isaac, Jacob).
usageNotes: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob are the three great patriarchs. David is also called a patriarch in Acts 2:29.

definition: The female head of a family or tribal line; a woman who is the founder or dominant figure of a family.
usageNotes: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, and Leah are considered the matriarchs of Israel. Their faith and actions shaped the nation's history.

definition: A person chosen by God to speak His messages to the people; one who declares divine truth, including but not limited to future events.
usageNotes: Prophets were God's mouthpieces, not merely fortune-tellers. They called people to repentance, declared God's will, and sometimes foretold the future.

definition: A female prophet; a woman chosen by God to declare divine messages.
usageNotes: Miriam, Deborah, Huldah, and Anna are named prophetesses. Philip the evangelist had four daughters who prophesied.

definition: An earlier term for a prophet; one who sees visions from God or perceives divine truth through spiritual insight.
usageNotes: 'He that is now called a Prophet was beforetime called a Seer.' Samuel was known as a seer before the term 'prophet' became standard.

definition: One sent out with a special commission; specifically, the twelve disciples chosen by Jesus plus Paul, who were sent to spread the gospel.
usageNotes: From Greek 'apostolos' (one sent). The twelve apostles were Jesus's chosen representatives. Paul claimed apostleship through a direct commission from the risen Christ.

definition: One who proclaims the good news (gospel); a preacher of the Christian message, especially to those who have not heard it.
usageNotes: Philip is called 'the evangelist.' Timothy is told to 'do the work of an evangelist.' Listed as a specific ministry gift.

definition: A servant or minister in the church; an appointed church officer who serves practical and spiritual needs of the congregation.
usageNotes: From Greek 'diakonos' (servant). The first deacons were appointed in Acts 6 to serve tables so the apostles could focus on prayer and teaching.

definition: An overseer or supervisor of a church congregation; a church leader responsible for pastoral care and governance.
usageNotes: From Greek 'episkopos' (overseer). In the New Testament, 'bishop,' 'elder,' and 'pastor' appear to describe the same role.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'give'. Archaic form meaning 'gives'.
usageNotes: The -eth ending indicates third person singular present tense. 'The LORD giveth and the LORD taketh away.'

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'say'. Archaic form meaning 'says'.
usageNotes: 'Thus saith the LORD' is the prophetic messenger formula introducing divine speech. One of the most recognizable KJV phrases.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'do'. Archaic form meaning 'does' or 'performs'.
usageNotes: 'Whatsoever he doeth shall prosper' means whatever he does will succeed. Standard -eth conjugation.

definition: Second person singular present tense of 'know'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you know'.
usageNotes: The -est ending is used with 'thou'. 'Thou knowest all things' is Peter's appeal to Jesus's omniscience.

definition: Second person singular present tense of 'do'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you do'.
usageNotes: Used in questions ('Why dost thou...?') and statements with 'thou.' Contracts 'doest' to 'dost.'

definition: Second person singular present tense of 'have'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you have'.
usageNotes: Contracts 'havest' to 'hast'. 'Thou hast the words of eternal life' is Peter's confession to Jesus.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'make'. Archaic form meaning 'makes'.
usageNotes: 'He maketh me to lie down in green pastures' from Psalm 23 is one of the most beloved verses in KJV.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'lead'. Archaic form meaning 'leads'.
usageNotes: 'He leadeth me beside the still waters' continues the shepherd imagery of Psalm 23.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'take'. Archaic form meaning 'takes'.
usageNotes: 'Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world' is John the Baptist's declaration about Jesus.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'come'. Archaic form meaning 'comes'.
usageNotes: 'Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord' was shouted at Jesus's triumphal entry into Jerusalem.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'live'. Archaic form meaning 'lives'.
usageNotes: 'As the LORD liveth' is a common oath formula meaning 'as surely as the LORD lives.' Used to swear by God's existence.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'love'. Archaic form meaning 'loves'.
usageNotes: 'Whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth' teaches that divine discipline is an expression of love.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'keep'. Archaic form meaning 'keeps'.
usageNotes: 'He that keepeth Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep' assures that God never stops watching over His people.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'seek'. Archaic form meaning 'seeks'.
usageNotes: 'There is none that seeketh after God' from Romans 3 describes humanity's natural spiritual condition.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'speak'. Archaic form meaning 'speaks'.
usageNotes: 'The mouth speaketh' out of the abundance of the heart—what we say reveals what is inside us.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'pass'. Archaic form meaning 'passes' or 'surpasses'.
usageNotes: 'The peace of God, which passeth all understanding' means God's peace surpasses human comprehension.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'remain'. Archaic form meaning 'remains'.
usageNotes: 'There remaineth therefore a rest to the people of God' promises a future sabbath rest for believers.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'endure'. Archaic form meaning 'endures' or 'lasts'.
usageNotes: 'His mercy endureth for ever' is repeated 26 times in Psalm 136, emphasizing the permanence of God's lovingkindness.

definition: Second person singular present tense of 'can'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you can'.
usageNotes: 'If thou canst believe, all things are possible' challenges the listener's faith capacity.

definition: Second person singular past tense of 'do'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you did'.
usageNotes: Used in questions and statements addressing one person about past actions. 'Why didst thou doubt?'

definition: Second person singular past tense of 'have'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you had'.
usageNotes: 'If thou hadst been here, my brother had not died' is Martha's and Mary's lament to Jesus about Lazarus.

definition: Second person singular past tense of 'will'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you would' or expressing a wish.
usageNotes: 'If thou wouldst believe' means 'if you would believe.' Used in conditional statements and polite requests.

definition: Second person singular conditional of 'shall'. Archaic form used with 'thou', meaning 'you should'.
usageNotes: Used in conditions and obligations directed at one person. 'That thou shouldst keep them from the evil.'

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'need'. Archaic form meaning 'needs'.
usageNotes: 'A workman that needeth not to be ashamed' describes one whose work is of such quality that no shame attaches to it.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'abide'. Archaic form meaning 'abides' or 'remains'.
usageNotes: 'Now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three' is Paul's conclusion to the love chapter, 1 Corinthians 13.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'walk'. Archaic form meaning 'walks'.
usageNotes: 'Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly' opens the Psalter with a wisdom teaching.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'sit'. Archaic form meaning 'sits'.
usageNotes: 'He that sitteth in the heavens shall laugh' portrays God's sovereign perspective above earthly plots.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'stand'. Archaic form meaning 'stands'.
usageNotes: 'Let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall' warns against spiritual overconfidence.

definition: Third person singular present tense of 'reign'. Archaic form meaning 'reigns' or 'rules'.
usageNotes: 'The LORD reigneth' is a joyful declaration of God's sovereign kingship found in several psalms.

definition: To belong to or be connected with as a rightful part; to pertain.
usageNotes: 'All that appertained to Korah' means everything belonging to Korah. Used of possessions, rights, or duties.

definition: A huge, powerful beast described in Job, often interpreted as a hippopotamus, elephant, or mythological creature symbolizing God's creative power.
usageNotes: Described in Job 40:15-24. God uses Behemoth to demonstrate His power over all creation. Its tail 'moveth like a cedar.'

definition: A great sea creature described in Job and Psalms, often interpreted as a crocodile, whale, or mythological sea dragon representing chaos.
usageNotes: Described extensively in Job 41. God uses Leviathan to show that only He can control the most fearsome creatures. Also mentioned in Psalm 74 and Isaiah 27.

definition: A valley, especially a broad one.
usageNotes: 'The king's dale' was a valley near Jerusalem where Absalom set up a pillar.

definition: A term found in the Psalms whose precise meaning is uncertain; possibly a musical or liturgical direction indicating a pause for reflection.
usageNotes: Appears 71 times in Psalms and 3 times in Habakkuk. May signal a musical interlude, a change in singers, or a moment for the congregation to reflect.

definition: An exclamation meaning 'Praise the LORD!' From Hebrew 'hallelu' (praise) and 'Yah' (the LORD).
usageNotes: Appears in many psalms as 'Praise ye the LORD.' The Greek form 'Alleluia' appears in Revelation 19. A universal expression of worship.

definition: An Aramaic expression meaning 'Our Lord, come!' or 'The Lord is coming!' A prayer for Christ's return.
modernEquivalent: Come, Lord! / The Lord is coming!
usageNotes: Used by Paul at the end of 1 Corinthians. One of the few Aramaic expressions preserved in the New Testament, showing it was a well-known early Christian prayer.

definition: A word expressing solemn agreement, affirmation, or endorsement. Means 'truly,' 'so be it,' or 'let it be so.'
modernEquivalent: truly / so be it / I agree
usageNotes: Used to affirm prayers, blessings, and statements. Jesus uniquely used 'Amen, amen' (verily, verily) to introduce His own authoritative statements.

definition: A fever, especially one characterized by fits of shivering; a malarial type of fever.
usageNotes: Listed among the curses for disobedience in Deuteronomy. 'The LORD shall smite thee with a consumption, and with a fever, and with an inflammation.'

definition: An archaic term for hemorrhoids or tumors. A plague afflicted the Philistines after they captured the Ark.
usageNotes: The Philistines were struck with emerods when they held the captured Ark of the Covenant, convincing them to return it.

definition: A cyst or abnormal growth on the body; a blemish that disqualified an animal from sacrifice.
usageNotes: Animals with wens or other blemishes could not be offered as sacrifices. Only unblemished animals were acceptable.

definition: A pestilence or plague affecting livestock, specifically an infectious disease of cattle.
usageNotes: The fifth plague of Egypt was a severe murrain that killed the Egyptians' livestock but spared the Israelites' animals.

definition: A mythical serpent hatched from a cock's egg; in KJV, likely refers to a venomous snake, possibly a cobra or viper.
usageNotes: Used in prophetic imagery: 'The sucking child shall play on the hole of the asp, and the weaned child shall put his hand on the cockatrice' den.'

definition: A plant with an extremely bitter taste; used figuratively for bitterness, sorrow, or calamity.
usageNotes: In Revelation, a star called 'Wormwood' falls and poisons the waters. Jeremiah uses it to describe the bitter consequences of sin.

definition: A bitter substance; bile. Used figuratively for bitterness, poison, or intense suffering.
usageNotes: Jesus was offered 'vinegar mingled with gall' at the crucifixion. 'The gall of bitterness' describes deep spiritual bondage.

definition: A catch of fish; also, a privy or latrine; also, a drink or the act of drinking.
usageNotes: Context determines meaning. 'A great draught of fishes' is a large catch. 'Goeth out into the draught' refers to a latrine.

definition: A container in which incense is burned, used in temple worship and priestly rituals.
usageNotes: The high priest used a golden censer on the Day of Atonement. In Revelation, an angel fills a golden censer with fire from the altar.

definition: A destructive larva or caterpillar that devours crops and vegetation.
usageNotes: Mentioned alongside locusts and palmerworms as agents of agricultural devastation. Joel describes their destruction as divine judgment.

definition: A type of caterpillar or locust larva that destroys vegetation. Part of the locust plague sequence.
usageNotes: Listed with the locust, cankerworm, and caterpillar as agents of crop destruction in Joel's prophecy.

definition: The womb; specifically used in the expression 'all that openeth the matrix' meaning the firstborn.
usageNotes: In KJV, 'matrix' always means womb. 'All that openeth the matrix' refers to the firstborn, who was consecrated to God.

definition: A young unmarried woman or girl.
usageNotes: Used throughout KJV for young women. 'The damsel is not dead, but sleepeth' is Jesus's statement before raising Jairus's daughter.

definition: A female servant or slave; used figuratively by a woman as a humble self-reference when speaking to a superior.
usageNotes: Mary called herself 'the handmaid of the Lord' in humble submission to God's plan. Hagar and Bilhah were handmaids.

definition: The master or head of a household; the husband or householder.
usageNotes: 'If the goodman of the house had known what hour the thief would come' uses this term for a householder in Jesus's parable.

definition: A broken piece of pottery or earthenware; a fragment of a clay pot.
usageNotes: Job scraped his boils with a potsherd. Used metaphorically: 'potsherds of the earth' describes fragile, worthless things compared to God.

definition: Strips of cloth wrapped tightly around a newborn infant to restrict movement, believed to aid proper growth.
usageNotes: 'Wrapped him in swaddling clothes' describes how Mary wrapped the newborn Jesus, a common practice for infants in the ancient world.

definition: Traveling on foot; journeying, especially as a stranger passing through.
usageNotes: 'A wayfaring man' is a traveler or stranger passing through. The parable of the Good Samaritan involves care for a wayfaring stranger.

definition: An ornament or band worn on the forehead; specifically, scripture passages bound between the eyes as commanded in Deuteronomy.
usageNotes: 'They shall be as frontlets between thine eyes' commands wearing God's words on the forehead, literally fulfilled as phylacteries.

definition: Past tense of 'outgo'; went ahead of or faster than; outpaced.
usageNotes: Used of crowds who ran ahead to reach Jesus's destination before Him. 'The people ran afoot thither out of all cities, and outwent them.'

definition: Lower; situated below or beneath; pertaining to the underworld or lower regions.
usageNotes: 'The nether parts of the earth' refers to the underworld or the depths below the surface. Also used of the lower millstone.

definition: Stupid, dull, or foolish, especially as a result of excessive drinking.
usageNotes: 'My people is foolish, they have not known me; they are sottish children' describes spiritual stupidity and ignorance of God.

definition: To grant or bestow something as a favor or privilege, often condescendingly or graciously.
usageNotes: Implies a gracious act by a superior toward an inferior. God vouchsafes His grace to the humble.

definition: Prostitution, sexual immorality, or spiritual unfaithfulness to God (idolatry metaphorically described as adultery).
usageNotes: Often used metaphorically by the prophets for Israel's idolatry. 'Going after other gods' is described as whoredom—spiritual adultery against God.

definition: An exclamation of grief, distress, or denunciation. As a noun, great sorrow or trouble.
usageNotes: Jesus pronounced 'woes' against the Pharisees and against cities that rejected Him. 'Woe unto you' is a solemn declaration of coming judgment.

definition: To detest, loathe, or regard with extreme disgust and hatred.
usageNotes: 'Abhor that which is evil; cleave to that which is good' instructs believers to have intense moral reactions against evil.

definition: Suffering, distress, or hardship; a state of pain, grief, or trouble.
usageNotes: 'I have seen the affliction of my people' is God's response to Israel's suffering in Egypt. Affliction often leads to spiritual growth.

definition: To punish by imposing a fine; to exact a monetary penalty.
usageNotes: Used of judges imposing fines as punishment. 'They shall amerce him in an hundred shekels of silver.'

definition: Formally promised or engaged to be married; bound by a marriage agreement.
usageNotes: In biblical culture, betrothal was legally binding and more serious than modern engagement. Mary was betrothed to Joseph when she conceived Jesus.

definition: A woman who lives with a man as a secondary wife without the full status of a wife, though recognized by law and custom.
usageNotes: Concubines had legal standing but fewer rights than primary wives. Abraham, Jacob, David, and Solomon had concubines. The practice is described but not endorsed.

definition: A gift or payment given by a man to the bride's family as part of the marriage arrangement; the bride price.
usageNotes: In biblical culture, the bridegroom paid a dowry (bride price) to the bride's father. Jacob worked 14 years as a dowry for Rachel and Leah.

definition: Lasting for a very short time; transient and fleeting.
usageNotes: Related to biblical imagery of life's brevity. 'For what is your life? It is even a vapour, that appeareth for a little time.'

definition: A crown or royal headband symbolizing sovereignty and authority.
usageNotes: Distinct from a victor's wreath. A diadem represents royal authority. In Revelation, Christ wears 'many diadems' showing supreme sovereignty.

definition: A round fruit with tough reddish skin and many seeds enclosed in juicy red pulp. A symbol of abundance and fertility in the Bible.
usageNotes: Pomegranates decorated the high priest's robe and Solomon's temple pillars. One of the fruits brought back by the spies from Canaan.

definition: A large tree of the fig family (Ficus sycomorus) common in ancient Palestine, producing edible fruit. Not the same as the modern sycamore.
usageNotes: Zacchaeus climbed a sycamore tree to see Jesus. Amos was 'a gatherer of sycomore fruit.' The biblical sycamore is a fig-mulberry tree.

definition: A thorny tree or shrub common in desert regions, whose durable wood was used in constructing the tabernacle and its furniture.
usageNotes: Called 'shittim wood' in KJV. The Ark of the Covenant, the table of showbread, and the altar were made of acacia wood overlaid with gold.

definition: The wood of the acacia tree, used extensively in the construction of the tabernacle and its furnishings.
usageNotes: Also a place name: the Israelites camped at Shittim before crossing the Jordan. The wood was valued for its durability in the desert climate.

definition: A bushy aromatic plant used in Jewish purification ceremonies, for sprinkling blood and water in ritual cleansing.
usageNotes: Used to apply blood to doorposts at the first Passover. David prayed 'Purge me with hyssop, and I shall be clean.' A sponge on hyssop was offered to Jesus at the cross.

definition: An aromatic resin obtained from certain trees, used in perfume, anointing oil, and embalming. One of the gifts of the Magi.
usageNotes: One of three gifts brought to the infant Jesus by the Magi, traditionally seen as symbolizing His suffering and death. Also used in the holy anointing oil.

definition: An aromatic gum resin burned as incense in worship, producing fragrant white smoke. One of the gifts of the Magi.
usageNotes: Used in temple worship and as a gift to the infant Jesus, symbolizing His deity. Part of the holy incense formula. The smoke represented prayers ascending to God.

definition: An expensive aromatic ointment derived from the root of the spikenard plant, native to India.
usageNotes: Mary of Bethany anointed Jesus's feet with costly spikenard. Judas objected to the expense. Also called 'spikenard' in KJV.

definition: A fragrant ointment made from the root of the nard plant, extremely costly and used for anointing.
usageNotes: A pound of spikenard was worth about a year's wages. Mary's extravagant anointing of Jesus was an act of deep devotion and prophetic preparation for burial.

definition: An aromatic bark similar to cinnamon, used in the holy anointing oil and as a perfume.
usageNotes: One of the ingredients in the sacred anointing oil prescribed in Exodus. 'All thy garments smell of myrrh, and aloes, and cassia.'

definition: An aromatic wood or resin used in perfumery and burial preparations, not the modern aloe vera plant.
usageNotes: Nicodemus brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes (about 75 pounds) to prepare Jesus's body for burial. Valued for its fragrance.

definition: A transparent, yellowish resin or possibly a precious stone. Its exact identification is uncertain.
usageNotes: Mentioned in the description of the Garden of Eden and in comparison to the appearance of manna.

definition: A precious stone, typically pale green, yellow, or blue. One of the stones in the high priest's breastplate.
usageNotes: One of the twelve stones on the high priest's breastplate representing the tribes of Israel. Also part of the foundation stones of the New Jerusalem.

definition: A precious gemstone, possibly a deep red garnet or ruby. One of the stones in the high priest's breastplate.
usageNotes: The exact stone is uncertain but likely a brilliant red gem. Part of the priestly breastplate and mentioned in descriptions of heaven.

definition: A reddish-brown precious stone, probably carnelian. The first stone in the high priest's breastplate.
usageNotes: One of the foundation stones of the New Jerusalem and a stone in the high priest's breastplate. Also describes the appearance of God on His throne.

definition: A precious blue gemstone. In biblical descriptions, may refer to lapis lazuli rather than the modern sapphire.
usageNotes: Under God's feet appeared 'a paved work of a sapphire stone.' Part of the priestly breastplate and the foundations of the New Jerusalem.

definition: An opaque precious stone of various colors. In Revelation, described as clear as crystal, probably referring to a diamond or crystal-clear gem.
usageNotes: The wall of the New Jerusalem is described as jasper. God's glory appeared 'like a jasper and a sardine stone.' May refer to a brilliant, transparent gem.

definition: A purple or violet variety of quartz, used as a gemstone. The twelfth stone in the high priest's breastplate.
usageNotes: Part of the priestly breastplate and the twelfth foundation of the New Jerusalem.

definition: A semi-precious stone with parallel bands of color, used on the high priest's shoulder pieces.
usageNotes: Two onyx stones engraved with the names of the twelve tribes were set on the shoulders of the high priest's ephod.

definition: The hardest stone; probably diamond or corundum. Used figuratively to mean unyielding or impenetrable.
usageNotes: God made Ezekiel's forehead 'harder than flint, even as an adamant stone' to withstand the opposition of rebellious Israel.

definition: Correctly; in the right way; properly.
usageNotes: 'Set not your heart aright' means did not direct your heart properly toward God.

definition: To lessen, ease, or calm; an archaic spelling of 'assuage'.
usageNotes: 'The waters asswaged' means the flood waters receded and calmed.

definition: A frame or platform on which a corpse or coffin is carried to the grave.
usageNotes: Jesus touched the bier of the widow of Nain's dead son and raised him to life.

definition: A coat of mail or body armor made of metal plates sewn onto fabric.
usageNotes: 'Put on the brigandines' is a call to arm for battle, putting on protective armor.

definition: A small earthenware container or jar used for holding oil or water.
usageNotes: The widow of Zarephath's cruse of oil did not fail during the famine, a miracle through Elijah.

definition: The sediment at the bottom of a cup of wine; the last, worst portion.
usageNotes: 'The dregs of the cup of trembling' represents drinking God's judgment to the very last drop.

definition: To preserve a dead body with spices and ointments to prevent decay.
usageNotes: Joseph commanded the physicians to embalm his father Jacob, following Egyptian custom. The process took forty days.

definition: To carve, cut, or etch letters or designs into a hard surface.
usageNotes: The Ten Commandments were engraved on stone tablets. The names of Israel's tribes were engraved on the priest's breastplate stones.

definition: Chains or shackles placed on the feet to restrain a prisoner.
usageNotes: The demoniac of Gadara broke his fetters and chains, showing supernatural strength from demonic possession.

definition: A plant whose fibers are used to make linen cloth. Also used of the wick of a lamp.
usageNotes: 'A bruised reed shall he not break, and smoking flax shall he not quench' describes the Messiah's gentleness with the weak.

definition: A person who cleans, thickens, and whitens cloth by beating and washing it.
usageNotes: At the transfiguration, Jesus's garments became whiter than any fuller could whiten them. Fuller's field was a landmark near Jerusalem.

definition: The act of gathering leftover grain or produce after the main harvest, a right granted to the poor.
usageNotes: Mosaic law required farmers to leave gleanings for the poor and foreigners. Ruth gleaned in Boaz's fields, leading to their marriage.

definition: One who goes ahead to announce or prepare the way for what follows; a forerunner.
usageNotes: John the Baptist was a harbinger of Christ, preparing the way of the Lord. The concept of a divine herald pervades messianic prophecy.

definition: The horizontal beam above a door or gateway.
usageNotes: At the first Passover, Israelites applied lamb's blood to the lintel and doorposts to protect their firstborn from the destroying angel.

definition: A bowl-shaped vessel in which substances are ground with a pestle; also, a building material for joining stones.
usageNotes: Manna was ground in mortars. The Israelites in Egypt were forced to make bricks with mortar (building material).

definition: A container or chest for storing the bones of the dead after the flesh has decayed.
usageNotes: Common in first-century Jewish burial practices. Bodies were placed in tombs, and after decomposition, bones were collected in ossuaries.

definition: The highest point of a building, especially the highest point of the temple in Jerusalem.
usageNotes: Satan tempted Jesus to throw Himself from the pinnacle of the temple, quoting Psalm 91 about angelic protection.

definition: A defensive wall or embankment surrounding a city or fortress.
usageNotes: 'Walk about Zion, and go round about her: tell the towers thereof. Mark ye well her ramparts' invites contemplation of God's protection.

definition: A musical instrument, likely a type of triangular stringed instrument or early form of trombone.
usageNotes: Listed among instruments played at Nebuchadnezzar's golden image dedication ceremony in Daniel.

definition: A seal or ring used to stamp an impression in wax or clay, authenticating a document or decree.
usageNotes: Pharaoh gave Joseph his signet ring, transferring authority to him. God says Zerubbabel will be 'as a signet' ring—precious and chosen.

definition: A trap for catching birds or animals; figuratively, anything that entices and entraps a person.
usageNotes: 'The fear of man bringeth a snare' warns that fear of human opinion traps people. Idols and false worship are frequently called snares.

definition: A bag made from animal skin used to store and transport wine.
usageNotes: Jesus taught that new wine must be put in new wineskins. Old wineskins become brittle and would burst from the pressure of fermenting new wine.

definition: A close companion or partner in work; one yoked together with another in shared labor.
usageNotes: Paul addresses a 'true yokefellow' in Philippians, asking this person to help resolve a conflict between two women in the church.
